prompt.content:
Use file “Cards_Patient 14” to access the entire medical history / record for the patient. Please use the patient tab as the source of truth for all demographic information. Disregard patient identifiers (Name, Age, DOB, MRN) found within the text of the clinicalnotes tab as they have been shifted for de-identification purposes

----

A 78-year-old gentleman presents to the emergency department with shortness of breath. 

His observations are as follows:
Heart rate - 77 bpm
Blood pressure - 110/60 mmHg
Oxygen saturations - 98% on room air
Respiratory rate - 16 bpm
temperature - 36.5 degrees C

The gentleman was started on an ACE inhibitor, but it was stopped due to high potassium. He still feels short of breath at times and has a digoxin allergy.He does not tolerate SGLT2 inhibitors.

What prognostically beneficial medication for chronic heart failure management is the most appropriate for this patient at this time? Please add the drug dose. Please follow the latest National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) guidelines.

golden_responses.golden_text_response:
The patient has symptoms such as shortness of breath, fatigue and swelling in the legs which all suggest heart failure. Echo confirms severe aortic stenosis with symptomatic heart failure. Since he still remains symptomatic despite the first line therapy, other options are considered. This is reccomended by NICE guidelines NG106. Options include:
Digoxin is excluded due to allergy and Angiotensin receptor blockers (ARBs)/ Angiotensin receptor neprolysin inhibitors(ARNIs)/Mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist (MRAs) are limited by hyperkalemia on ACE (Angiotension concerting enzyme) inhibitor. Hydralazine and nitrate therapy is avoided in severe aortic stenosis and would likely worsen hypotension. Ivabradine would be the best choice, given his heart rate is > 75 bpm and sinus, also his ejection is <35%. The starting dose would be 2.5 mg twice daily for two weeks.
